title: Medigap Plan N vs. Plan G: Which Medicare Supplement Is Right for You?
rawContent:
What Is Medigap Plan N and How Does It Work?

Most Medicare beneficiaries are surprised to learn that Original Medicare leaves significant cost-sharing gaps, and that a standardized supplement policy can close most of them for a predictable monthly premium. Medigap Plan N is one of those policies. Regulated by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), it is a Medicare Supplement insurance plan available to anyone enrolled in both Medicare Parts A and B.

Because Plan N is standardized, the benefits it provides are identical no matter which insurance carrier sells it. A Plan N from Humana covers the same items as a Plan N from Mutual of Omaha. What differs between carriers is the monthly premium, the rate increase history, and the customer service experience, not the coverage itself.

The central trade-off with Plan N is straightforward: you get broad protection against large medical bills at a meaningfully lower monthly premium than more comprehensive plans, in exchange for accepting modest, predictable cost-sharing through copays and two uncovered items. Understanding exactly what those gaps are, and whether they matter for your situation, is the key to deciding if Plan N is the right fit.

Medicare Supplement Plan N 2026: Coverage Details and Copays

Plan N covers a wide range of Medicare cost-sharing expenses. Here is what the plan pays in 2026:

  • Medicare Part A deductible: $1,736 per benefit period, fully covered

  • Part A hospital coinsurance: $434 per day for Days 61–90 and $868 per day for lifetime reserve days, fully covered

  • Skilled nursing facility (SNF) coinsurance: $217 per day for Days 21–100, fully covered

  • Part B coinsurance: 100% covered after the annual deductible, minus applicable copays

  • Foreign travel emergency: 80% of costs after a deductible, up to plan limits

  • Hospice care coinsurance: Fully covered

Two important items are not covered by Plan N. First, the 2026 Medicare Part B deductible of $283 you pay this out of pocket each year before Plan N's coinsurance coverage activates. Second, Medicare Part B excess charges are not covered. These are additional amounts that providers who do not accept Medicare assignment can legally bill above the Medicare-approved rate.

Plan N also carries a copay structure that Plan G does not. You may pay up to $20 for doctor office visits and up to $50 for emergency room visits that do not result in inpatient hospital admission. These copays are what allow Plan N to offer lower premiums, your cost exposure stays predictable, but it is not zero.

When comparing Medigap Plan G and Plan N, most of the benefits are identical. The differences come down to three specific items. The table below makes those distinctions clear.

The premium difference is significant. According to the American Association for Medicare Supplement Insurance (AAMSI), Plan N premiums typically run 15–25% lower than Plan G for the same enrollee profile. Jesse Slome, Executive Director of AAMSI, notes: "Plan N is one of the more competitively priced Medigap options on the market today, agents are recommending it more frequently to clients who want broad protection but are sensitive to monthly premium costs."

Understanding Part B Excess Charges and Why They Matter for Plan N

One of the most misunderstood aspects of Plan N involves Medicare Part B excess charges. When a provider does not accept Medicare assignment, meaning they have not agreed to accept Medicare's approved rate as full payment, they can legally charge up to 15% above the Medicare-approved amount. Plan N does not cover that additional 15%.

Plan G does.

Here is a practical example. If Medicare approves $500 for a specialist visit and the provider does not accept assignment, they can bill up to $575. Plan N would leave you responsible for that $75 difference. Across multiple specialist visits, these charges can add up over the course of a year.

The good news is that excess charges are completely prohibited in several states, including Ohio, Connecticut, Massachusetts, Minnesota, New York, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, and Vermont. If you live in one of these states, this gap in Plan N essentially does not exist, making Plan N an even more attractive option there.

For beneficiaries in other states, the practical solution is straightforward: confirm that your doctors accept Medicare assignment before your appointments. Medicare.gov offers a physician finder tool that lets you verify assignment status by provider. Choosing assignment-only providers largely eliminates excess charge exposure.

How Much Does Medigap Plan N Cost Per Month in 2026?

Plan N premiums vary depending on your age, gender, state of residence, tobacco use status, and the rating method your insurer uses, community-rated, issue-age-rated, or attained-age-rated. As a general range, a 65-year-old non-smoking woman might pay roughly $80–$130 per month for Plan N, while a 70-year-old man in the same area could see premiums closer to $110–$170 per month. These figures shift considerably by state and carrier.

To put the premium difference into concrete terms, consider this scenario. Assume Plan G costs $150 per month and Plan N costs $115 per month for the same enrollee, a $35 monthly difference, or $420 annually. Now calculate Plan N's potential out-of-pocket costs in 2026:

  • Part B deductible: $283 (paid once per year)

  • Office visit copays: 6 visits × $20 = $120

  • Total additional Plan N costs: $403

In this scenario, Plan N's additional out-of-pocket costs ($403) are nearly offset by the annual premium savings ($420), meaning both plans cost roughly the same for a moderate healthcare user. If you visit the doctor fewer than 6 times per year and avoid excess charges, Plan N likely saves you money. If your usage is higher, Plan G may offer better overall value.

The State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) offers free, unbiased help comparing Plan N premiums from carriers in your specific area, a resource worth using before you enroll.

Who Should Choose Medigap Plan N vs. Plan G?

Plan N is likely a strong fit if you:

  • Are relatively healthy with moderate doctor visit frequency

  • Want protection from major hospital bills without paying for coverage you rarely use

  • Live in a state that prohibits excess charges, or plan to use Medicare-assignment providers exclusively

  • Are budget-conscious and want to put the monthly premium savings toward other retirement expenses

Plan G may be the better choice if you:

  • Have chronic conditions requiring frequent specialist visits

  • Want zero cost-sharing beyond the annual Part B deductible

  • Live in a state with high concentrations of non-assignment providers

  • Prefer simplicity over calculating copay exposure each year

Juliette Cubanski, Deputy Director of the Program on Medicare Policy at the Kaiser Family Foundation, explains the growing appeal: "Medigap Plan N has grown in popularity because it offers near-comprehensive coverage at a lower premium, which appeals to healthier beneficiaries who are willing to accept modest cost-sharing in exchange for savings."

Patricia Barry, author of Medicare For Dummies, adds: "For people who rarely see doctors and want protection from catastrophic costs, Plan N strikes a good balance, the copays are predictable and capped, so your risk exposure remains manageable."

When and How to Enroll in Medigap Plan N

Timing your Medicare Supplement open enrollment correctly is one of the most consequential decisions you will make. Your Medigap Open Enrollment Period is a 6-month window that begins the month you are both age 65 or older and enrolled in Medicare Part B. During this period, insurance companies cannot deny you Plan N coverage, charge you more due to health conditions, or make you wait for coverage to begin.

Outside this window, most states allow insurers to apply medical underwriting. That means you could be denied a Plan N policy, or charged a significantly higher premium, based on pre-existing conditions. This is a risk that many people overlook when they assume they can always switch plans later.

There are some exceptions. Guaranteed issue rights allow you to enroll in or switch to Plan N without underwriting in specific situations, such as losing employer-sponsored coverage, or if a Medicare Advantage plan exits your service area. State-specific rules also vary, some states like New York and Connecticut offer year-round guaranteed issue protections regardless of circumstance.

Your action steps are clear: confirm your Part B enrollment date, use that date to identify your Open Enrollment window, compare Plan N premiums from multiple carriers, and apply before the window closes. Working with a licensed independent Medicare agent or contacting SHIP ensures you do not inadvertently miss this critical period.

How to Find the Best Medigap Plan N Companies

Since Plan N benefits are identical across all carriers standardized by CMS, the coverage you receive is the same regardless of which insurer you choose. Your evaluation criteria should shift entirely to price, rate stability, financial strength, and service quality.

One of the most important, and often overlooked, factors is an insurer's premium rate increase history over the past 3–5 years. A carrier offering the lowest initial premium may apply aggressive annual increases, making it more expensive over time than a carrier that started slightly higher but remained stable. Your state insurance department publishes rate filings that are publicly accessible.

AAMSI and CMS enrollment data are useful tools for understanding which carriers have significant market presence and track records. You can also check financial stability ratings from agencies like A. M. Best. For unbiased, no-cost guidance, reviewing top Medicare supplement companies and consulting SHIP counselors in your state are both excellent starting points.

Working with a licensed independent agent, rather than a captive agent who represents only one carrier, gives you access to a broader range of Plan N quotes and objective carrier comparisons. As Mary Beth Donahue, President and CEO of the Better Medicare Alliance, has noted, supplemental coverage plays an increasingly important role in financial security as Medicare out-of-pocket costs rise.

Frequently Asked Questions About Medigap Plan N

What does Medigap Plan N cover in 2026?

Plan N covers the Part A deductible ($1,736), Part A hospital coinsurance, skilled nursing facility coinsurance ($217/day), 100% of Part B coinsurance after the deductible (minus applicable copays), foreign travel emergency care at 80%, and hospice coinsurance. It does not cover the Part B deductible ($283) or Part B excess charges.

What is the difference between Medigap Plan G and Plan N?

There are three differences: Plan G covers the $283 Part B deductible and Part B excess charges, Plan N covers neither. Plan N adds office visit copays (up to $20) and ER copays (up to $50) that Plan G does not charge. In exchange, Plan N premiums run 15–25% lower than Plan G for the same enrollee.

How much does Medigap Plan N cost per month?

Premiums vary by age, gender, state, and the carrier's rating method. A 65-year-old non-smoking woman might pay roughly $80–$130 per month depending on location, while older enrollees typically pay more. Compare quotes through a licensed agent or your state's SHIP program for accurate local pricing.

Does Medigap Plan N cover the Part B deductible?

No. Plan N does not cover the 2026 Medicare Part B deductible of $283. You pay this amount once per calendar year before Plan N's coinsurance coverage applies. This is one of the two key gaps that separates Plan N from Plan G, per the CMS standardized benefit chart.

Does Plan N cover Medicare Part B excess charges?

Plan N does not cover Part B excess charges. Providers who do not accept Medicare assignment can bill up to 15% above the Medicare-approved rate, and that additional cost falls on you. To avoid this, verify your providers accept Medicare assignment, or consider Plan G if you regularly see non-assignment specialists.

Who should choose Medigap Plan N?

Plan N suits relatively healthy beneficiaries who want protection from large hospital and medical bills but are comfortable with modest, capped copays. It is particularly attractive for those in states that prohibit excess charges and for budget-conscious enrollees who want to reduce their monthly premiums without sacrificing broad coverage.

Can I switch from Plan G to Plan N later?

In most states, switching from Plan G to Plan N outside a guaranteed issue period requires passing medical underwriting, approval is not guaranteed. If you have developed health conditions since first enrolling, you could be denied. This makes the initial Open Enrollment Period the best time to choose carefully. Consult a licensed agent before making any switch outside a protected window.

Choosing between Medigap Plan N and Plan G comes down to one core question: how much predictable cost-sharing are you willing to accept in exchange for lower monthly premiums? For many Medicare beneficiaries, Plan N offers an excellent balance, broad protection from catastrophic costs with manageable copays and real premium savings. For others who prioritize zero cost-sharing and see multiple specialists regularly, Plan G delivers greater peace of mind.

The most important step you can take right now is to compare Medigap plan options during your Open Enrollment Period, before health underwriting becomes a factor. Speak with a licensed independent Medicare agent or contact your state's SHIP program for free, personalized guidance, and make your decision with current 2026 figures in hand, not estimates.
